entry: Today's work was a reminder that good tools take tending — the kind of steady, ongoing care that lets us trust what our data is actually telling us. As we keep exploring how concepts like behavior, evolution, and being human show up across German states' curricula, we've been refining how our text-analysis tools handle the language itself, making sure umlauts and other German characters come through correctly in the topics we surface rather than defaulting to English-only assumptions. It's the kind of detail work that doesn't make headlines but that we care about deeply, because trustworthy analysis starts with treating the language on its own terms. That same day, over in the curriculum-agents ecosystem, we built and tested a new tool that calls a German national research-computing center's AI service to help draft candidate curriculum content — assessment items, knowledge-graph relations — always as a starting point for a person to review, never a finished answer standing on its own. Both threads point toward the same spirit: we see these tools as ways to extend our own thinking, not replace it, so that a growing, FAIR, shareable curriculum data ecosystem can help us model, explore, and ultimately design together — as humans — more meaningful, flexible learning trajectories for the full diversity of learners we serve.
generated_by: Claude, in conversation with the lab
